title: Measurement
objective: Students will explore measurement concepts including Fractions on a number line, Telling time to the minute (age 9+), Converting measurement units (age 9+) and more.

steps.content: Have you ever thought about Fractions on a number line? Let's find out together!
Solve word problems involving distances, time intervals, liquid volumes, masses, and money using the four operations with fractions or decimals; represent with diagrams including number lines
Let's practice together! A 2.5 kg bag of flour is split equally into 4 portions — what does each weigh?
If you is planning a 4.5 km walk and has already walked 1.75 km, can they work out how far is left — and estimate how long the remaining walk might take?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Fractions on a number line: A 2.5 kg bag of flour is split equally into 4 portions — what does each weigh? A journey takes 1 hr 45 min; what time do you arrive if you leave at 09:20?
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Telling time to the minute (age 9+)!
Solve problems involving converting between units of time (hours↔minutes, minutes↔seconds, years↔months, weeks↔days)
Let's practice together! Convert 3 hours 25 minutes to 205 minutes
If you needs to convert 2 hours 35 minutes into just minutes, can they do it — and then convert 180 seconds into minutes and seconds?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Telling time to the minute (age 9+). Convert 3 hours 25 minutes to 205 minutes A programme lasts 150 seconds — express in minutes and seconds
Let's discover the amazing world of Converting measurement units (age 9+)!

Know relative sizes of measurement units within one system (km/m/cm/mm, kg/g, l/ml, hr/min/sec); convert between different metric units and express measurements in terms of a smaller unit; record equivalents in conversion tables
Let's practice together! Convert 3.5 km to 3,500 m
If you needs to convert 3.5 kg to grams, can they do it confidently — and also fill in a simple two-column table showing the same measurements in kg and g?
Amazing work! Let's review what we discovered about Converting measurement units (age 9+). Convert 3.5 km to 3,500 m Complete a conversion table for cm and mm: (1,10), (2,20), (3,30)...
Let's discover the amazing world of Estimating answers (age 9+)!

Apply the area formula (l × w) and perimeter formula (2l + 2w) for rectangles including squares in real-world and mathematical problems; calculate and compare areas using standard units (cm², m²) and estimate areas of irregular shapes
Time to try this out: Find the width of a room given area = 48 m² and length = 8 m
If you wants to carpet a room 6.5 m × 4 m, can they calculate the area — and also work out how many metres of skirting board are needed around the edge?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Estimating answers (age 9+): Find the width of a room given area = 48 m² and length = 8 m Calculate the area of a square with side 7.5 cm
Let's explore Measurement Line Plots! Have you ever wondered about this?
Make a line plot to display measurement data in fractions of a unit (1/2, 1/4, 1/8); solve problems involving addition and subtraction of fractions using line plot data
Let me show you something cool. Create a line plot of seed growth measurements in 1/8-inch increments
If you records caterpillar lengths as fractions of an inch and plots them on a number line, can they work out the total length of three caterpillars by adding the fractions from the plot?

Great job! Today we learned about Measurement Line Plots. Create a line plot of seed growth measurements in 1/8-inch increments Use a line plot to find the difference between the longest and shortest specimens
Welcome to an exciting lesson about Metric & Imperial Conversion!
Understand and use approximate equivalences between metric units and common imperial units (inches, pounds, pints)
Time to try this out: State that 1 inch ≈ 2.5 cm and use this to estimate length in inches
If you hears that a baby weighs 7 pounds, can they give a rough idea of that in kilograms — using the approximate conversion they know?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Metric & Imperial Conversion: State that 1 inch ≈ 2.5 cm and use this to estimate length in inches Know that 1 kg ≈ 2.2 pounds and estimate a person's weight in pounds
Let's discover the amazing world of Perimeter of Compound Shapes!
Measure and calculate the perimeter of composite rectilinear shapes in centimetres and metres
Let's practice together! Find the perimeter of an L-shaped figure by identifying all side lengths
If you is given an L-shaped garden plan with all the side measurements labelled, can they add all the sides together to find the total perimeter?

You did it! Here's what we explored about Perimeter of Compound Shapes: Find the perimeter of an L-shaped figure by identifying all side lengths Calculate the perimeter of a composite shape where some sides must be deduced
Let's discover the amazing world of Estimating volume!
Estimate volume of cuboids using 1 cm³ blocks; estimate capacity of containers using water
Time to try this out: Build a cuboid from 1 cm³ blocks and state its volume
If you has a small cardboard box, can they estimate how many 1 cm³ cubes would fit inside it — and then fill it with sugar cubes to check?
You did it! Here's what we explored about Estimating volume: Build a cuboid from 1 cm³ blocks and state its volume Estimate how many cm³ blocks would fill a given box
